title: Advanced Economic Theory and Policy
description: A comprehensive examination of macro and microeconomic principles, history, and policy interactions.

questions.text: An economy is operating at full employment. The government increases spending financed by selling bonds to the public, while the Central Bank maintains a strict inflation target. What is the most likely long-term result?
options: A permanent increase in real GDP via the multiplier effect.
Complete crowding out of private investment due to rising real interest rates.
A shift in the composition of GDP from investment to public consumption with no change in total output.
A decrease in the velocity of money as defined by the Equation of Exchange.

questions.text: In the context of the 'Lucas Critique,' why might traditional econometric models fail to predict the effects of a change in economic policy?
options: The models fail to account for the irrationality of consumer behavior.
The parameters of the models are not structural and change when policy regimes change because agents adjust expectations.
The models rely too heavily on micro-foundations rather than aggregate data.
The models do not account for the time-lag between fiscal and monetary implementation.

questions.text: A good is classified as a 'Giffen Good' if, following a price increase, the:
options: Substitution effect is positive and outweighs the negative income effect.
Income effect is positive and reinforces the substitution effect.
Substitution effect is negative, but the negative income effect for an inferior good is stronger.
Demand curve shifts to the right due to a change in consumer preferences.

questions.text: According to the Ricardian Equivalence proposition, a tax-cut financed by government debt will:
options: Stimulate aggregate demand by increasing current disposable income.
Have no effect on aggregate demand because taxpayers anticipate future tax increases to pay off the debt.
Lead to a 'Liquidity Trap' as interest rates fall to zero.
Increase the money supply as the government prints currency to cover interest.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a movement along the supply curve rather than a shift in the supply curve?
options: An improvement in production technology.
An increase in the price of a substitute in production.
An increase in the market price of the good itself caused by an increase in demand.
A decrease in the cost of raw materials.

questions.text: In a Liquidity Trap, the LM curve is:
options: Perfectly vertical, making monetary policy highly effective.
Upward sloping, reflecting the Fisher Effect.
Perfectly horizontal, making monetary policy ineffective at lowering interest rates.
Downward sloping, suggesting a paradox of thrift.

questions.text: Which school of thought emphasizes that 'shocks' to technology and productivity are the primary causes of the business cycle, rather than changes in aggregate demand?
options: Monetarist School
Keynesian School
Real Business Cycle (RBC) Theory
Austrian School

questions.text: The 'Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis' suggests that in the long run, the price of primary commodities:
options: Increases relative to manufactured goods due to scarcity.
Declines relative to manufactured goods, leading to a deterioration in terms of trade for developing nations.
Remains stable due to the law of one price.
Is determined solely by the gold standard.

questions.text: If the Central Bank engages in 'Sterilization' after an unsterilized intervention in the foreign exchange market, it is trying to:
options: Increase the volatility of the exchange rate.
Offset the effect of the foreign exchange intervention on the domestic monetary base.
Force the government to reduce its fiscal deficit.
Prevent the domestic currency from depreciating.

questions.text: Which of the following occurs when a tax is placed on a good with perfectly inelastic demand?
options: The producer bears the entire burden of the tax.
The consumer bears the entire burden of the tax, and there is no deadweight loss.
The deadweight loss is maximized because consumption does not change.
The supply curve shifts vertically downward.

questions.text: Say’s Law, a pillar of Classical Economics, is best summarized as:
options: Demand creates its own supply.
Supply creates its own demand.
Prices and wages are 'sticky' in the short run.
The velocity of money is always constant.

questions.text: During the 'Great Moderation' (mid-1980s to 2007), what was the primary characteristic of the global economy?
options: Hyperinflation in developed nations.
Low volatility in business cycles and stable, low inflation.
The collapse of the Bretton Woods system.
A return to strict mercantilist policies.

questions.text: The Phillips Curve originally suggested a trade-off between inflation and unemployment. Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps argued that in the long run:
options: The trade-off is even steeper.
There is no trade-off, and the curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
Expected inflation is always zero.
Lower unemployment can only be achieved by increasing the money supply indefinitely.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an 'Automatic Stabilizer' in fiscal policy?
options: A legislative decision to build a new interstate highway system.
The Central Bank lowering the federal funds rate.
A progressive income tax system that collects less revenue during a recession.
An emergency stimulus check issued by the Treasury.

questions.text: The 'Fisher Effect' states that a 1% increase in the expected inflation rate will lead to:
options: A 1% decrease in the nominal interest rate.
No change in the nominal interest rate.
A 1% increase in the nominal interest rate, keeping the real interest rate constant.
A 1% increase in the real interest rate.

questions.text: If the cross-price elasticity of demand between Good A and Good B is -1.5, this implies that the goods are:
options: Strong substitutes.
Strong complements.
Unrelated (independent).
Both inferior goods.

questions.text: A 'Monetarist' would argue that the most effective way to manage the economy is to:
options: Adjust tax rates frequently to manage demand.
Maintain a steady, predictable growth rate of the money supply.
Use discretionary interest rate hikes to prevent any stock market bubble.
Nationalize major industries to ensure full employment.

questions.text: What does the 'Impossible Trinity' (Trilemma) in international economics state that a country cannot have simultaneously?
options: Low inflation, high growth, and low unemployment.
A fixed exchange rate, free capital movement, and an independent monetary policy.
A trade surplus, high domestic consumption, and low taxes.
Fiscal surplus, monetary easing, and a gold standard.

questions.text: The 'Taylor Rule' is a formula used to suggest how:
options: Governments should set tax rates based on GDP growth.
Central Banks should adjust nominal interest rates in response to inflation and output gaps.
Firms should price their goods to maximize profit.
The velocity of money relates to the price level.

questions.text: Which phenomenon occurs when short-term interest rates are higher than long-term interest rates on government bonds?
options: Contango
Normal Yield Curve
Inverted Yield Curve
Liquidity Preference

questions.text: In New Keynesian Economics, 'Menu Costs' refer to:
options: The physical cost of printing new menus in a restaurant.
The costs firms face when changing prices, which leads to price stickiness.
The cost of food inflation in the CPI.
The inefficiency of government subsidies in the agricultural sector.

questions.text: What is the primary cause of 'Cost-Push Inflation'?
options: Excessive growth in the money supply.
A decrease in the aggregate supply due to rising input costs (e.g., oil prices).
An increase in consumer confidence leading to higher spending.
A government budget surplus.

questions.text: Under the 'Gold Standard,' if a country had a trade deficit, what was expected to happen according to the Price-Specie Flow Mechanism?
options: It would borrow more gold from the IMF.
Gold would flow out, the money supply would contract, prices would fall, and exports would become more competitive.
The currency would be devalued by the central bank.
The government would increase tariffs to stop the flow of gold.

questions.text: The 'Paradox of Thrift' suggests that if everyone tries to save more during a recession:
options: Capital investment will increase, leading to a faster recovery.
Aggregate demand will fall, potentially reducing total savings and income.
Inflation will skyrocket as banks have more money to lend.
The multiplier effect will become infinite.

questions.text: Which of the following would cause a 'Change in Demand' (a shift in the curve) for electric vehicles?
options: A decrease in the price of electric vehicles.
An increase in the price of gasoline (a complement-in-use for ICE vehicles).
A decrease in the cost of lithium-ion batteries.
The introduction of a new assembly line robot that speeds up production.

questions.text: What is 'Seigniorage'?
options: The process of banks lending out more than they hold in reserves.
The profit made by a government by issuing currency, especially the difference between the face value and production cost.
A tax on imported luxury goods.
The interest rate charged by the central bank to commercial banks.

questions.text: In the Mundell-Fleming model with perfect capital mobility and a floating exchange rate, expansionary fiscal policy is:
options: Highly effective in increasing GDP.
Ineffective because it leads to currency appreciation and a decrease in net exports.
Effective only if accompanied by a tax increase.
The only way to reduce unemployment.

questions.text: Which concept explains why a person might continue to watch a boring movie just because they paid $15 for the ticket?
options: Marginal Utility
Sunk Cost Fallacy
Opportunity Cost
The Law of Diminishing Returns

questions.text: Stagflation is defined as a period of:
options: High inflation and high economic growth.
Low inflation and high unemployment.
High inflation and high unemployment/stagnant growth.
Negative inflation and negative growth.

questions.text: According to the Coase Theorem, private parties can solve the problem of externalities without government intervention if:
options: Taxes are high enough to discourage the externality.
Property rights are well-defined and transaction costs are low.
The government mandates a specific level of production.
The externality is positive rather than negative.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between the CPI (Consumer Price Index) and the GDP Deflator?
options: CPI measures only food and energy, while GDP Deflator measures all goods.
CPI includes imported goods, while the GDP Deflator only includes domestically produced goods.
GDP Deflator uses a fixed basket of goods, while CPI does not.
There is no difference; they always yield the same inflation rate.

questions.text: The 'Hysteresis' effect in labor markets suggests that:
options: Wages always adjust quickly to clear the market.
Long periods of high unemployment can increase the natural rate of unemployment as workers lose skills.
Inflation is always a monetary phenomenon.
Minimum wage laws always create a surplus of labor.

questions.text: In a Cournot Competition model, firms compete by setting:
options: Prices.
Quantities.
Advertising budgets.
Quality standards.

questions.text: The 'Laffer Curve' illustrates the relationship between:
options: Inflation and Unemployment.
Tax rates and total Tax Revenue.
Interest rates and Investment.
Money Supply and GDP.

questions.text: If a Central Bank engages in 'Quantitative Easing' (QE), it is primarily:
options: Lowering the reserve requirement for small banks.
Buying long-term assets (like government bonds) to inject liquidity and lower long-term interest rates.
Selling gold to strengthen the currency.
Increasing the discount rate to fight inflation.

questions.text: A 'Natural Monopoly' occurs when:
options: The government grants an exclusive patent to a firm.
One firm can supply the entire market at a lower cost than two or more firms due to massive economies of scale.
A firm owns all the natural resources required for production.
The demand for a product is perfectly inelastic.

questions.text: The 'Pigouvian Tax' is designed to:
options: Redistribute wealth from the rich to the poor.
Internalize the costs of a negative externality.
Provide a subsidy for essential goods.
Ensure that the government budget is always balanced.

questions.text: What does the 'Gini Coefficient' measure?
options: The rate of inflation over time.
The degree of income inequality within a population.
The efficiency of a country's tax system.
The volatility of the stock market.

questions.text: In the 'Solow-Swan Growth Model,' what is the primary driver of long-term sustainable growth in per capita income?
options: Capital accumulation (saving more).
Population growth.
Exogenous technological progress.
Increasing the labor force participation rate.

questions.text: The 'Dutch Disease' refers to a situation where:
options: An increase in agricultural exports leads to a famine.
A large discovery of natural resources leads to currency appreciation, making other sectors (like manufacturing) uncompetitive.
The government spends too much on social welfare, leading to high debt.
A country becomes too dependent on imports from the Netherlands.

questions.text: What is 'Menu Cost' in the context of inflation?
options: The cost of food increasing in restaurants.
The cost to firms of changing their prices, such as reprinting catalogs or updating systems.
The cost to consumers of searching for the lowest price.
The loss of purchasing power for people on fixed incomes.

questions.text: The 'Law of One Price' is the basis for which economic theory?
options: Keynesian Multiplier
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
The Phillips Curve
Comparative Advantage

questions.text: If the income elasticity of demand for a good is 0.5, the good is a:
options: Luxury good.
Normal good (Necessity).
Inferior good.
Giffen good.

questions.text: What was the 'Bretton Woods System'?
options: A system of free-floating exchange rates established after WWII.
A system of fixed exchange rates where currencies were pegged to the US Dollar, which was pegged to gold.
The first international agreement to eliminate all tariffs.
The plan that established the Euro as a common currency.

questions.text: In a 'Prisoner's Dilemma' game, the 'Nash Equilibrium' usually results in:
options: The best possible outcome for both players.
An outcome where both players cooperate.
An outcome where both players defect (betray), which is worse than if they had both cooperated.
A situation where one player wins and the other loses everything.

questions.text: The 'Haavelmo Theorem' (Balanced Budget Multiplier) suggests that if the government increases spending and taxes by the same amount:
options: GDP will not change.
GDP will increase by the amount of the spending increase (Multiplier = 1).
GDP will decrease because taxes discourage work.
Inflation will increase by 10%.

questions.text: What is 'Shoeleather Cost'?
options: The cost of replacing shoes worn out from walking to the store.
The resources wasted when people change their behavior to avoid holding cash during high inflation.
The tax on leather goods.
The cost of transporting goods over long distances.

questions.text: Which of the following is most likely to cause 'Demand-Pull Inflation'?
options: A sudden spike in global oil prices.
An increase in the corporate tax rate.
Excessive government spending during a period of full employment.
A decrease in consumer confidence.

questions.text: A 'Veblen Good' is a product for which demand increases as:
options: Consumer income decreases.
The price increases, because it is seen as a status symbol.
The price of substitutes decreases.
The government provides a subsidy.

questions.text: The 'Tragedy of the Commons' occurs with goods that are:
options: Excludable and Rivalrous.
Non-excludable and Rivalrous.
Excludable and Non-rivalrous.
Non-excludable and Non-rivalrous.

questions.text: What does the 'Equation of Exchange' (MV = PY) suggest if Velocity (V) and Output (Y) are constant?
options: An increase in the Money Supply (M) will lead to a proportional increase in the Price Level (P).
An increase in the Money Supply will decrease interest rates.
Fiscal policy is more effective than monetary policy.
The government should always run a deficit.

questions.text: Which economist is most associated with the idea of 'Creative Destruction'?
options: Adam Smith
John Maynard Keynes
Joseph Schumpeter
Friedrich Hayek

questions.text: A central bank that follows a 'Hard Peg' exchange rate policy essentially gives up:
options: The ability to collect taxes.
Its control over domestic interest rates (Independent Monetary Policy).
Its ability to trade with other nations.
Its seat at the World Bank.

questions.text: If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8, the simple spending multiplier is:
The 'Heckscher-Ohlin Model' of trade predicts that countries will export goods that:
options: Are made by the most skilled workers only.
Intensively use their relatively abundant factors of production (e.g., land, labor, capital).
Have the highest price on the global market.
Are produced by state-owned enterprises.

questions.text: What is the 'Real Interest Rate' if the nominal interest rate is 5% and the inflation rate is 7%?
Which type of unemployment is considered 'voluntary' or a natural part of a healthy economy where people are between jobs?
options: Structural Unemployment
Cyclical Unemployment
Frictional Unemployment
Seasonal Unemployment

questions.text: The 'J-Curve' effect in international trade suggests that after a currency depreciation, the trade balance will:
options: Immediately improve.
Immediately worsen before improving in the long term.
Improve for exports but worsen for imports indefinitely.
Remain unchanged.

questions.text: A 'Pure Public Good' is characterized by being:
options: Rivalrous and Excludable.
Non-rivalrous and Non-excludable.
Provided only by the private sector.
Cheap to produce but expensive to sell.

questions.text: In the context of the 'Production Possibilities Frontier' (PPF), an inward shift of the curve represents:
options: Economic growth.
A decrease in the economy's total productive capacity (e.g., due to war or natural disaster).
A change in consumer tastes from one good to another.
An increase in the unemployment rate.

questions.text: The 'Efficiency Wage Theory' suggests that firms pay higher-than-market wages because:
options: They are forced to by powerful unions.
It increases worker productivity and reduces turnover.
The government mandates it through the minimum wage.
They want to increase the rate of inflation.

questions.text: Which policy action would a 'Keynesian' recommend during a deep recession with high unemployment and low inflation?
options: Reducing government spending to balance the budget.
Increasing interest rates to attract foreign capital.
Increasing government deficit spending to stimulate aggregate demand.
Decreasing the money supply to prevent future inflation.

questions.text: The 'Tobin Tax' is a proposed tax on:
options: Real estate transactions.
Short-term foreign exchange transactions (currency speculation).
Carbon emissions.
High-income earners.

questions.text: What does 'Adverse Selection' refer to in insurance markets?
options: When people take more risks because they have insurance.
When high-risk individuals are more likely to purchase insurance than low-risk individuals, leading to a distorted market.
When the government forces everyone to buy insurance.
When insurance companies go bankrupt.

questions.text: The 'Kuznets Curve' hypothesizes that as a country develops, economic inequality:
options: Decreases steadily.
Increases steadily.
First increases and then decreases.
Remains constant.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Monetary Neutrality'?
options: Changes in the money supply have no effect on real variables (like GDP) in the long run, only on nominal variables (like prices).
The Central Bank should not take sides in political elections.
Interest rates should always be 0%.
The government should never borrow from the central bank.

questions.text: A 'Contractionary Monetary Policy' involves:
options: Increasing government spending.
Decreasing the money supply and increasing interest rates.
Decreasing taxes.
Buying government bonds on the open market.

questions.text: The 'Golden Rule' level of capital in the Solow model is the steady-state level of capital that maximizes:
options: Total Output.
Consumption per worker.
The savings rate.
Population growth.

questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Flow' variable rather than a 'Stock' variable?
options: The National Debt.
The Wealth of a household.
The Annual Budget Deficit.
The amount of money in a savings account.

questions.text: The 'Marshall-Lerner Condition' relates to:
options: The effectiveness of a tariff in reducing imports.
Whether a currency devaluation will improve the trade balance based on the price elasticities of exports and imports.
The relationship between tax rates and work effort.
The impact of education on wage growth.

questions.text: In a 'Bertrand Competition' model with identical products, firms compete by setting:
options: Quantities.
Prices.
Output quotas.
Research and development targets.

questions.text: What is 'Moral Hazard'?
options: Buying insurance because you are sick.
Changing your behavior to be more risky because you are protected from the consequences (e.g., by insurance or a bailout).
The ethical dilemma of charging high prices for medicine.
The risk of a stock market crash.

questions.text: The 'Marginal Rate of Substitution' (MRS) represents the:
options: Rate at which a consumer is willing to trade one good for another while maintaining the same level of utility.
The cost of producing one more unit of a good.
The change in total revenue from selling one more unit.
The rate at which the government replaces one tax with another.

questions.text: What was the 'Price Revolution' of the 16th century largely attributed to?
options: The Black Death.
The influx of silver and gold from the Americas into Europe.
The invention of the steam engine.
The start of the Napoleonic Wars.

questions.text: According to 'Rational Expectations' theory, people make economic decisions based on:
options: Past events only (Adaptive expectations).
All available information, including predictions about future government policy.
Random guesses.
Guidance from the government only.

questions.text: An 'Okun's Law' relationship describes the correlation between:
options: Inflation and interest rates.
Changes in GDP growth and changes in the unemployment rate.
The money supply and the price level.
The tax rate and government revenue.

questions.text: In the 'AS-AD Model,' a 'Positive Supply Shock' (like a fall in oil prices) leads to:
options: Higher prices and lower output.
Lower prices and higher output.
Higher prices and higher output.
Lower prices and lower output.

questions.text: The 'Linder Hypothesis' suggests that countries with similar per capita incomes will:
options: Never trade with each other.
Trade manufactured goods with each other because they have similar preference patterns.
Go to war over resources.
Only trade primary commodities.

questions.text: What is 'Hyperinflation' typically defined as (Cagan's definition)?
options: Inflation exceeding 10% per year.
Inflation exceeding 50% per month.
Inflation exceeding 100% per year.
Any inflation caused by government spending.

questions.text: In the 'Long Run Aggregate Supply' (LRAS) curve, the level of output is determined by:
options: The price level.
The amount of money in circulation.
Productive capacity (factors of production and technology).
The nominal interest rate.

questions.text: The 'Quantity Theory of Money' is most closely associated with which economist?
options: John Maynard Keynes
Milton Friedman
David Ricardo
Thomas Malthus

questions.text: Which of the following would increase the 'Natural Rate of Unemployment'?
options: A decrease in the minimum wage.
An increase in the power of labor unions.
An increase in the demand for labor.
A decrease in unemployment benefits.

questions.text: A 'Tax Wedge' refers to the difference between:
options: Income of the rich and income of the poor.
What an employer pays an employee and what the employee receives after taxes.
The tax rate in the US vs. Europe.
Direct taxes and indirect taxes.

questions.text: The 'Absence of Double Counting' in GDP means that:
options: We only count the value of final goods, not intermediate goods.
We do not count the sale of used goods.
We do not count transfer payments (like Social Security).
All of the above.

questions.text: In the 'Standard Trade Model,' a 'Terms of Trade' improvement occurs when:
options: A country's export prices rise relative to its import prices.
A country's export prices fall relative to its import prices.
The country stops trading entirely.
The country implements a trade deficit.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of the 'M1' money supply?
options: Currency in circulation.
Demand deposits (checking accounts).
Traveler's checks.
Certificate of Deposits (CDs) / Time deposits.

questions.text: What does 'Pareto Efficiency' mean?
options: Everyone in society has an equal amount of wealth.
A situation where no one can be made better off without making someone else worse off.
The government has balanced its budget.
The economy is growing at 5% per year.

questions.text: The 'Rent-Seeking' behavior refers to:
options: Trying to find a cheap apartment.
Spending resources to gain wealth by lobbying the government for special favors or monopolies rather than creating new wealth.
The act of charging interest on loans.
Landlords increasing rent during a housing shortage.

questions.text: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Monopolistic Competition'?
options: A single seller.
Identical products.
Many sellers with differentiated products.
Barriers to entry are insurmountable.

questions.text: If a government uses 'Expansionary Fiscal Policy' while the economy is at its 'Potential Output,' the most likely result is:
options: A permanent increase in real output.
Inflation and a return to the original level of real output in the long run.
A decrease in the national debt.
A decrease in nominal interest rates.

questions.text: The 'Discount Rate' is the interest rate:
options: Banks charge their best customers.
The Central Bank charges commercial banks for short-term loans.
Paid on government bonds.
Used to calculate the present value of future cash flows.

questions.text: According to the 'Absolute Advantage' theory by Adam Smith, trade is beneficial because:
options: It allows countries to specialize in what they produce most efficiently and swap for other goods.
It forces other countries to become dependent.
It allows the government to collect more taxes.
It prevents inflation.

questions.text: What is 'Deadweight Loss'?
options: The loss of life during a recession.
The reduction in total surplus (consumer + producer) that results from a market distortion such as a tax.
The amount of debt a country cannot pay back.
The cost of disposing of unsold goods.

questions.text: A 'Capital Account' (or Financial Account) surplus means a country is:
options: Exporting more goods than it imports.
A net borrower from the rest of the world (capital is flowing in).
A net lender to the rest of the world.
Increasing its gold reserves.

questions.text: Which of the following would lead to an increase in the 'Velocity of Money'?
options: An increase in the frequency with which workers are paid.
A decrease in the use of credit cards.
Higher interest rates (increasing the opportunity cost of holding cash).
Both A and C.

questions.text: The 'Inside Lag' of policy refers to:
options: The time it takes for a policy to affect the economy once implemented.
The time it takes for policymakers to recognize a problem and implement a policy.
The time it takes for the public to notice a change in taxes.
The time between two consecutive recessions.

questions.text: In a 'Regressive Tax' system:
options: High-income earners pay a higher percentage of their income in tax.
Low-income earners pay a higher percentage of their income in tax.
Everyone pays the same dollar amount.
The tax rate decreases as the economy grows.

questions.text: The 'Income Effect' of a wage increase for a worker might lead them to:
options: Work more hours because the opportunity cost of leisure has risen.
Work fewer hours because they can now afford more leisure while maintaining their standard of living.
Quit their job immediately.
Demand a lower tax rate.

questions.text: Which of the following is the 'Twin Deficits Hypothesis'?
options: The idea that a government budget deficit leads to a current account (trade) deficit.
The idea that inflation and unemployment always move together.
The idea that high taxes and high spending both cause recessions.
The idea that the US and China both have deficits.

questions.text: What is 'Forward Guidance' in monetary policy?
options: Telling the government what laws to pass.
Communication by the Central Bank about the intended future path of interest rates to influence market expectations.
Setting the exchange rate for the next five years.
A strategy used to prevent banks from failing.
